speaker: interviewee
text: --to walk. And I walked past a customer of my father's who was a hatter in Hodslavice. I walked in there. By this time, I needed some lunch and something to drink. And the man was one of these Czechs, you know, who became a German, and these borderland people who were not reliable. He also owed us money. I didn't know that he owed us money. I don't know what he thought, but he wouldn't even give me a glass of water. But I got-- I found a pump and I, I walked to Novy Jicin. And it was all familiar country, you know. The walk was-- I like walking countryside.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I walk into the city. I walk to our house, straight to our house, and I see there's a German family living there. The house had been redecorated completely, looked much nicer, actually, the way it was redecorated. But the tiles were still the same in the-- in the hall. The tiles were still there in 1994 when I went there last. And my brothers weren't there. So I asked if there were any other people who returned. And the only family was a family, Bermann, who were in Theresienstadt, and came back from Theresienstadt to Novy Jicin. So I stayed the night with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the only ones that came back were the Mannheimer brothers. They were neighbors of ours. And [INAUDIBLE] Mannheimer was Milush's best friend. They were working on a farm somewhere in a field. So I walked there, found them. And I asked, you know, have you seen Leo and Milush? And I think that really was the most difficult moment for me. When, in the beginning of July, when everybody who should have been back was back, and even I, who was one of the late comers, came back if they hadn't turned up, they were dead. And that's-- I sort of felt, you know, now I was alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was one other address of Czech friends of ours in Ostrava, this city where we have the house-- we have, we don't have it. I went to visit them to Ostrava. And I get out of the station into the main road and who should I bump into, but Yehuda Backon, so big hello.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you could go to every city. You showed your number at the town hall, and they gave you 1,000 crowns so that you could live for a week-- just sort of welfare. Without any papers or anything, they just gave you cash. So he said, have you have you got your 1,000 crowns yet? And it was 10:00 in the morning, so I said no. So he said, well, I'm just off to get my 1,000 crowns. And then we went to the town hall.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was telling me he's going on Youth Aliyah. And he's just come to his home town, which saw Ostrava. And that he was staying in a chateau in Bohemia, near Prague and all the boys are there. And he named the people who had returned.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, well, I need to rejoin my father and I don't know. I can write him letters, but every time that he tries to get in touch with me, I'm moving about, you know? I was in Austria. I sent a card. And I was in Wiener Neustadt, and I sent a card. And I don't know whether these things arrived. So he said, well, there's a transport of children from Theresienstadt to England. Why don't you go to Stirin and talk to Premysl Pitter. The fellow who ran it on behalf of the Ministry of Welfare-- social services in Czechoslovakia-- was a Protestant preacher called Premysl Pitter. So he said, why don't you go to Prague, visit the, the chateau, and talk to Pitter. So that night instead of staying in Ostrava, I took a train to Prague. And the train was unbelievably crowded-- obviously a passenger train, but unbelievably crowded. I fought my way onto the footstep. And I was standing on this footstep and started moving off. And I thought I can't stand on the last footstep of this train all the way to Prague, you know? It's-- you die of exposure or something. So I clambered up onto the roof of the-- of the wagon. And I was traveling in the open air. And it's rather eerie when you go through tunnels, you know, because you have to really duck because there's this wall whizzing by you. Anyway, I arrive in Prague, nicely black from soot, you know, from this journey. It took a long time. And now where do you go in Prague?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I walked down the main square called Vaclavske Namesti, and there was a customer of my parent's, my father's, with whom-- whom I had met during this time in 1941 when Leo had me for a week in Prague. And I found this hatter, this lady. It was a sort a swanky store with women's hats, you know, height of fashion, carpets. And I was this dirty boy who walks in from-- straight from the station. This was the first idea I had.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I asked her if she remembers me. She didn't recognize me. So I said, I'm Misa Honigwachs, Leo's brother. And she's a Christian woman. She says, Jesus Maria, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and she says, where is Leo? So I said to her, I was going to ask you whether Leo is in Prague? Because Leo would have been in touch with her because they were good friends. So she says, I got to get you cleaned up.

speaker: interviewee
text: She started shutting up shop. She took me, and she explained, I'm taking you to my husband's apartment. I've left my husband. And now I've got a boyfriend who is a pilot in the Royal Air Force, and he's stationed here in Prague. But I'm not living with my husband, but the only place that I know where you can get a bath and where you can stay the night is my flat where I lived with my husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: And these were really lovely people-- not only she, but these two forgot their marital problem and for him, there was no question, you know? He started running the hot water. And that-- and so that's how you got to England?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well then, we started-- I had a bath and supper. And over supper in the kitchen-- I didn't tell them this whole story because it's takes, takes ten reels or whatever. I said, I've got to get to England. I don't know where my father is, but once I get to England, I'll find him. I only have this Swiss address.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the husband says to his wife, what about your boyfriend, the pilot? Why doesn't he take him on his flight, you know, just take him without papers to England? So she said, I don't think he'll do it. But she said she'd talk to him. And then she, she disappeared.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he-- this man was full of good sense, you know, sort of-- he said, well, we can't telephone because the telephones don't work yet-- you know, international telephone-- we'll send a telegram to your address to tell your father where you are. Perhaps a telegram will reach him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And next morning, you must-- tomorrow morning, you must go to the Ministry of Interior and ask who is-- no, you must to go to the English Embassy. They'll know about this transport of children. So I went to the embassy and they looked at me like mad, you know? Do you know your, your father's address? Of course, I didn't. I told them this story. And I was told politely, you know, find your father's address, then come back to us. But meanwhile-- and they knew nothing about this transport of children. Can you just tell me very briefly what happened please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the next thing that-- the next idea was that I should go through the Ministry of Interior and ask, you know, who is organizing the transport of children from Terezin to England? So I go to the Ministry of Interior and it's all new, you know. Prague had only just been liberated. I walk into the building, and the man at the gate asks me what I want. And I explain, and he says, hmm. And he tells me like this-- it's in colloquial Prague Czech, so it's very funny the way he said it. Because he says, look, this place is full of people. They've all just come from all over the place and nobody knows what anybody else is doing in this building. I don't even know where to send you. So he says, look, it's got six floors, this building. Start on the sixth floor and go into every room and ask the officials in the room the question you're asking.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I worked my way through every room. I got to the first floor where the senior officials were already. There were sort of minister secretaries. Then I was beginning to knock on doors. And I walk into a room of a man who-- and I explain what I want. And he says, what did you say your name was? And he says, I know your father. Well, he says, I don't know him. My wife knows him. And you're the brother of Leo, aren't you? So I said, how do you know Leo? So he said, well, I went to university with Leo. He was in the medical faculty, and I was in literature and history. And I took a law degree, and he took a medical degree. And we knew each other very well. So I ask him, what is your wife's name? And he says, Stella. So I said, Do you mean Stella Fuchs? He had married Leo's girlfriend, Stella Fuchs. And the man into whose office I had blundered was a man called Viktor Fischl, who has become Avigdor Dagan and now writes books, not about the Holocaust. His brother writes about the Holocaust. They're both very good, very good literary figures, particularly Avigdor, or Viktor. So Victor says, look, I'll telegraph my wife. And I've got a letter here somewhere that I should be looking out for you. And his desk was covered with papers. And he starts rummaging amongst the papers. He says, it's hopeless, but I know about this transport, and don't worry. Give me your address. Come and see me every day because I'll get you on to this transport.

speaker: interviewee
text: And for a month I kept visiting Viktor at the office there. And I lived with Mr. Novotny, and I waited for these bombers to come from England to bring the Czech Air Force to take the children from Theresienstadt to, to England. And it took a month-- from mid-July to mid-August. We arrived in Windemere on the 15th of August 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you see your father after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next day. There's a story-- amongst the stories that I gave you, there's a story about how the psychologist who was in charge of the whole operation in Windemere thought that me meeting my father would be a great shock to me. I, I, I don't know. Don't talk to me about psychologists and the logic of Freudian psychology. There's a whole-- it's quite an involved article about the Freudians. But she thought that I would suffer some sort of trauma, so she had my father wait in a waiting room. And she was questioning me for half an hour, thinking that I could have some sort of delirium, you know, if I met my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after about half an hour's conversation I said her, for heaven's sakes, what are these questions for? Why are you asking me all this? I mean, get to the point. So finally she says, well, the point is your father is in the room next door. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was completely exasperated with this woman. And that was this Alice Goldberger, who was such a good woman, you know, looked after the children. There were-- there were little babies. Read the article. It's really quite--

speaker: interviewee
text: Alice Goldberger then reminded me of my mother-- you know, that image of a do-good person who blunders by waiting-- by making my dad wait for another half an hour after I hadn't seen him since 1939. I mean, it's idiotic.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened about your schooling?

speaker: interviewee
text: My dad, bless him, was quite a man. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, I threw myself into learning English. And I threw myself into taking matriculation. I sort of did three years work in one year. I joined Habonim. I became a youth leader, went to summer camps in France with Habonim. In 1948, Habonim said, we want to train you as a youth leader. And we want to send you to Israel. Israel was only just a new state. And I had a promised place at King's College already. So I wrote to King's College whether I could defer my start for-- by-- for a year. And they said, yes. And I went to Israel. And that was the best year of my life. It was wonderful. I stayed at this place which now is a university called Beit Berl. It's still called Beit Berl near Kfar Saba. And I was with Dutch people from, from Habonim and with English people, American, South African.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the only one who had gone through a Hol-- through the Holocaust. All the Dutch were people who returned to Holland from the West, you know? And we had some marvelous courses-- mainly Hebrew. Learning Hebrew was so exciting. And the countryside, and the freedom, and meeting my brothers, you know, the two brothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: '48-- I hadn't seen them for nine years. It's like a lifetime. It seemed then like, like a lifetime. And Kfar Ruppin was such an exciting place where they lived in Emek Beit She'an. It's all tropical with date palms and fish ponds. And then they had small children there. You know, they were both married by that time. It was very-- I spent weekends going to them, hitchhiking to Kfar Ruppin. And I would get as far as Beit She'an and then walk. And I walked into the night. And they said, for heaven's sakes, you know there are Arabs roaming the countryside. Somebody will kill you on the way. Next time you must telephone and we'll send a truck for you. Go into the police station in Beit She'an if you're stuck, and we'll, we'll come for you. But I walked. I, I loved-- I loved this walk through this tropical countryside and then coming to this compound of Kfar Ruppin. And they already knew this, this crazy brother of the Hons.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were there for a year. What happened when you came back here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went to Kings College. I took an engineering degree. I took an apprenticeship, professional apprenticeship, with English Electric. And I went to work with English Electric in the aircraft industry because I wanted to get-- to do something useful in Israel. And then I met Eve, who is an Israeli. And her parents live in Haifa. So I thought, oh, well, I want to go to Israel. This law will make it much easier. But when it came to the crunch, Eve said, it's better for us to stay in England.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1955. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I had a career with various companies. Eventually I built chemical plants. I built-- in 1964, I built the largest tonnage oxygen plant in Germany, of all places. And I kept flying into Germany, to Dusseldorf, once a week, more or less. At a moment's notice, I'd take a plane, hire a car, run about, come back. I recall, there was a big advertisement as you walked into the airport from the customs hall, there was a big advertisement of the map of Germany, the way it used to be-- you know, the Great Reich under Hitler. And it said-- the advertisement said, av drie Teile geteitl, nie. Divided into three parts, never. And the three parts was West Germany, East Germany, and then the Oder-Neisse part, which Poland took away. And I always looked at that, and I thought to myself, let it stay the way it is, you know, divided. Germany is better divided. And now that I visit Czechoslovakia again, and I've got a bit of a better grasp of history-- since my retirement I have been doing more history than engineering. In fact, I don't do any engineering. I think to myself that the unification of Germany has been a wrong move.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you just tell me about the reunion with the other Birkenau Boys that happened recently, in recent years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the last reunion was '94. '90 was the first reunion. And somehow the first reunion was a better atmosphere than the last one. I advised on the last one that we should take a hotel in the countryside. And we should stay there for several days and just be amongst us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there were three television crews that came because several other Birkenau Boys with their own initiatives arranged. And that was terribly intrusive because people behave differently under the camera. So I felt that I wasn't getting through to, to people, you know, on a people level. They were all acting parts. We're in this nice hotel in the countryside, but Tommy Frankel, for example, who is a Birkenau Boy, somehow smelled it, you know, what was going to happen. He himself is a camera man and worked in, in TV. And he worked as a director and has made films. Quite a tal-- I've seen some of his films. He's made a film about Theresienstadt, as well. I think he gave evidence. He told me that he gave evidence to you. Tommy said, from our room, the [INAUDIBLE] party, we will stay down river at a different hotel, which is more secluded. And you can't get there by car except for a big detour, and we're not going to have any television cameras. And every morning I took a stroll down to my friends from [INAUDIBLE], whom I knew better. And we had a nice, sort of-- we were friends again, like we were in public school together in Theresienstadt. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And there was Karl Langer and Tommy Frankel and Paul Laupert and Otto Jacubowicz, myself--

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll have a look at the photograph in a moment. Pavel, Pavel Kohn. They are lovely guys. When were your children born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Daniel was born in '56, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just over a year after we got married. We were married, and we were staying in St. Annes-on-Sea. I was finishing my engineering apprenticeship. And I was working on the P1 Lightning Fighter, and I could see that the future in the aircraft industry for a Jew, you know, amongst secret work in the Ministry of Defense was not the atmosphere for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was married, and Eve says one evening-- I mean, we were using stuff, you know, not-- because we were young. Eve was only 18. She's says, I don't think I can get pregnant, you know? So we tried, [LAUGHS] and, of course, bang, she got pregnant. So then we didn't have any friends there. And I said, we're going back to London, and I'm going to give up the aircraft industry. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we get back to London and I got a job with a company making industrial models of chemical plants. And Eve was-- became highly pregnant. Her parents came.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married in Haifa, actually, because we traveled to Haifa for the wedding and then came back. We had a honeymoon traveling across Greece, and Italy, and France, back to England. I finished my apprenticeship. I resigned my job in-- at Warton Aerodome and came back to London. And then I started working for this company making tonnage oxygen plants. And then I started building some very large projects. I left them and worked for another company. Jonathan was born about five years later, 1961. Then we bought a house. And because of going to college in London and because of my activities in Habonim, I had lots of London friends. So Eve suddenly had a sort of young group of people around her. Other people, you know, old girlfriends were in the same situation-- children.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we just stayed in England and kept visiting Israel often. And I know Hebrew so-- from this course that I did in 1948. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a sort of happy family life, except Eve herself is a survivor. But she can't talk about it. She can't bring myself to. She's told me some stories, horrendous stories, as well. They were in hiding for five years. But she was two when the war started. And she was eight when the war was over. And, to her, it's sort of colored by her mother's attitude. Her mother is also in hysterics about their time in hiding. Their father-- her father, he, he said he would write it down one day. I keep urging him, but he-- the family is sort of against it. The women are against it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I thank you very much for telling us your story today.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.
